Chemistry of Life.skill: SYI-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) Chemistry of Life - SYI 1.1 Structure of Water and Hydrogen Bonding
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic covers the structure of water and hydrogen bonding for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that water's polarity arises from the unequal sharing of electrons between oxygen and hydrogen, creating partial charges that enable hydrogen bonding. Key properties of water include high specific heat, high heat of vaporization, cohesion, adhesion, surface tension, and its role as a versatile solvent for polar and ionic substances. Water's density anomaly (ice floats) is biologically critical for aquatic ecosystems. Students must explain how hydrogen bonds, though individually weak, collectively create emergent properties essential for life. Connect to membrane transport, enzyme function, and temperature regulation in organisms. AP exam note: expect questions linking water's molecular properties to biological phenomena such as transpiration, thermoregulation, and buffer systems. Vocabulary: polarity, hydrogen bond, cohesion, adhesion, specific heat, heat of vaporization, solvent, hydrophilic, hydrophobic.

Chemistry of Life.skill: ENE-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Chemistry of Life - ENE Elements of Life
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic covers the elements essential for life for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that living organisms are composed primarily of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur (CHONPS). Carbon's ability to form four covalent bonds makes it the backbone of organic molecules. Trace elements such as iron, magnesium, and zinc play critical roles as cofactors in enzymatic reactions. Students must recognize that the properties of elements arise from their atomic structure and electronegativity, which determine bonding behavior and molecular shape. Connect to biological macromolecules, energy transfer via ATP (phosphorus), and protein structure (sulfur in disulfide bonds). AP exam note: questions may ask students to explain why carbon is central to life's chemistry or to identify the role of specific elements in biological processes. Vocabulary: organic molecule, covalent bond, electronegativity, trace element, cofactor, functional group.

Chemistry of Life.skill: SYI-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Chemistry of Life - SYI Introduction to Biological Macromolecules
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic introduces biological macromolecules for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand that macromolecules are polymers built from monomers through dehydration synthesis (condensation) reactions and broken apart by hydrolysis. The four classes are carbohydrates (monosaccharides), proteins (amino acids), nucleic acids (nucleotides), and lipids (which are not true polymers but include fatty acids and glycerol). Each class has distinct monomers, bonding patterns, and functional roles. Students must be able to identify monomers and describe how polymerization and hydrolysis involve the removal or addition of water. Connect to enzyme-catalyzed reactions in digestion and biosynthesis, and to energy storage and structural roles. AP exam note: free-response questions frequently ask students to compare and contrast macromolecule classes. Vocabulary: monomer, polymer, dehydration synthesis, hydrolysis, carbohydrate, protein, nucleic acid, lipid.

Chemistry of Life.skill: SYI-1.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Chemistry of Life - SYI 1.4 Properties of Biological Macromolecules
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic covers properties of biological macromolecules for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand how the structure of each macromolecule class determines its properties and functions. Carbohydrates serve as energy sources (glucose, starch, glycogen) and structural components (cellulose, chitin). Lipids function in long-term energy storage, membrane structure (phospholipids), and signaling (steroids). Proteins have diverse roles including catalysis, transport, defense, and structure. Nucleic acids store and transmit genetic information. Students must explain how R-group interactions in amino acids determine protein folding and function. Connect to how changes in pH, temperature, or environment can disrupt macromolecule structure (denaturation). AP exam note: questions often require interpreting molecular diagrams and predicting function from structure. Vocabulary: saturated, unsaturated, phospholipid, amino acid, R-group, polypeptide, denaturation.

Chemistry of Life.skill: SYI-1.D (Unit 1, Topic 1.5) Chemistry of Life - SYI 1.5 Structure and Function of Biological Macromolecules
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic covers the structure-function relationship of biological macromolecules for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-1.D, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand the four levels of protein structure: primary (amino acid sequence), secondary (alpha helices and beta sheets from hydrogen bonds), tertiary (3D folding from R-group interactions), and quaternary (multiple polypeptide subunits). The sequence of amino acids determines the shape, and shape determines function. Students must understand that changes in amino acid sequence (mutations) can alter protein shape and function, as seen in sickle cell disease. Carbohydrate structure (alpha vs. beta glycosidic linkages) determines digestibility and function (starch vs. cellulose). Connect to enzyme specificity, membrane proteins, and gene expression. AP exam note: expect questions on how environmental changes denature proteins. Vocabulary: primary through quaternary structure, alpha helix, beta sheet, disulfide bond, glycosidic linkage, substrate specificity.

Chemistry of Life.skill: IST-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.6) Chemistry of Life - IST 1.6 Nucleic Acids
Chemistry of Life.prompt: This topic covers nucleic acids for AP Biology students (CED: IST-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand the structure and function of DNA and RNA. Nucleotides consist of a phosphate group, a five-carbon sugar (deoxyribose in DNA, ribose in RNA), and a nitrogenous base. DNA uses adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine; RNA replaces thymine with uracil. DNA forms a double helix with antiparallel strands joined by complementary base pairing (A-T with 2 hydrogen bonds, G-C with 3). RNA is typically single-stranded and exists as mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA. Students must explain how the structure of DNA enables replication and information storage. Connect to gene expression (transcription and translation), mutations, and biotechnology. AP exam note: questions may require comparing DNA and RNA or explaining how nucleic acid structure relates to function. Vocabulary: nucleotide, phosphodiester bond, complementary base pairing, antiparallel, double helix, purine, pyrimidine.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: SYI-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) Cell Structure and Function - SYI Cell Structure: Subcellular Components
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers subcellular components for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles including the nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum (rough and smooth), Golgi apparatus, mitochondria, chloroplasts (in plants), lysosomes, and vacuoles. Each organelle has a specific structure that supports its function: the nucleus houses DNA, ribosomes synthesize proteins, mitochondria perform cellular respiration, and chloroplasts carry out photosynthesis. Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles but contain ribosomes, a cell membrane, and genetic material in a nucleoid region. Students must compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Connect to the endomembrane system, endosymbiotic theory, and cell compartmentalization. AP exam note: questions frequently ask about organelle function and how structure supports that function. Vocabulary: organelle, eukaryote, prokaryote, endomembrane system, ribosome, cytoplasm, nucleoid.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: SYI-2.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) Cell Structure and Function - SYI 2.2 Cell Structure and Function
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers cell structure and function for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that all cells share common features: a plasma membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, and genetic material. Eukaryotic cells are distinguished by internal membrane-bound compartments that allow specialized functions. The endomembrane system (ER, Golgi, vesicles, plasma membrane) works together to produce, modify, and transport proteins and lipids. The cytoskeleton (microfilaments, intermediate filaments, microtubules) provides structural support, facilitates movement, and aids in cell division. Students must explain how subcellular structures contribute to cell function and how disruption of any component affects the whole cell. Connect to cell communication, transport, and division. AP exam note: diagrams of cells with labeled organelles are common. Vocabulary: cytoskeleton, microfilament, microtubule, endomembrane system, vesicle, centriole, flagellum, cilium.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.3 Cell Size
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers cell size for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that cell size is limited by the surface area-to-volume ratio. As a cell grows, its volume increases faster than its surface area, reducing the efficiency of nutrient uptake and waste removal across the membrane. This explains why cells remain small or develop adaptations (folding, elongation, microvilli) to increase surface area. Students must calculate and compare surface area-to-volume ratios for different cell shapes and sizes, and explain why larger organisms have more cells rather than larger cells. Connect to metabolic rate, diffusion efficiency, and why mitochondria and chloroplasts have folded internal membranes. AP exam note: mathematical reasoning about SA:V ratios appears frequently. Vocabulary: surface area-to-volume ratio, diffusion, microvilli, metabolic demand.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.4 Plasma Membranes
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers plasma membranes for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand the fluid mosaic model: the membrane is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins, cholesterol, glycoproteins, and glycolipids. Phospholipids are amphipathic, with hydrophilic heads facing aqueous environments and hydrophobic tails forming the interior. Membrane proteins include integral (transmembrane) and peripheral proteins that function in transport, signaling, enzymatic activity, and cell recognition. Cholesterol modulates membrane fluidity across temperature ranges. Students must explain how membrane composition enables selective permeability. Connect to cell signaling, immune recognition, and membrane transport. AP exam note: questions may ask students to predict how changes in membrane composition affect function. Vocabulary: fluid mosaic model, phospholipid bilayer, amphipathic, integral protein, peripheral protein, glycoprotein, cholesterol, selective permeability.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.5 Membrane Permeability
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers membrane permeability for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that the plasma membrane is selectively permeable: small nonpolar molecules (O2, CO2) and water cross freely, while ions and large polar molecules require transport proteins. Permeability depends on molecule size, polarity, and charge. The hydrophobic core of the bilayer acts as a barrier to charged and polar substances. Students must predict which substances can cross the membrane by simple diffusion and which require facilitated transport. Connect to osmosis, facilitated diffusion, and active transport mechanisms. AP exam note: experimental design questions may involve dialysis tubing or artificial membranes to test permeability. Vocabulary: selective permeability, concentration gradient, simple diffusion, hydrophobic, hydrophilic, polar, nonpolar, electrochemical gradient.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.D (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.6 Membrane Transport
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers membrane transport for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand the distinction between passive transport (no energy required, moves down concentration gradient) and active transport (requires ATP, moves against concentration gradient). Passive transport includes simple diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion. Active transport uses membrane pumps such as the sodium-potassium pump (Na+/K+ ATPase). Bulk transport includes endocytosis (phagocytosis, pinocytosis, receptor-mediated) and exocytosis. Students must compare transport mechanisms and predict the direction of molecular movement given concentration data. Connect to nerve impulse transmission, kidney function, and cellular energetics. AP exam note: questions frequently involve interpreting transport diagrams and predicting outcomes. Vocabulary: passive transport, active transport, endocytosis, exocytosis, concentration gradient, ATP, pump, vesicle.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.E (Unit 2, Topic 2.7) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.7 Facilitated Diffusion
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers facilitated diffusion for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.E, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that facilitated diffusion is passive transport mediated by channel proteins or carrier proteins. Channel proteins form hydrophilic pores for ions and small polar molecules (e.g., aquaporins for water). Carrier proteins undergo conformational changes to shuttle specific molecules across the membrane. This process requires no ATP and moves substances down their concentration gradient. Transport rate is limited by the number of available proteins (saturation kinetics). Students must explain how protein specificity enables selective transport and distinguish facilitated diffusion from simple diffusion and active transport. Connect to glucose transport, ion channels in neurons, and enzyme-like specificity. AP exam note: graphs showing transport rate vs. concentration may test understanding of saturation. Vocabulary: channel protein, carrier protein, aquaporin, saturation, specificity, conformational change.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.F (Unit 2, Topic 2.8) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.8 Tonicity and Osmoregulation
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers tonicity and osmoregulation for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.F, Unit 2, Topic 2.8). Students should understand that tonicity describes the relative solute concentration of a solution compared to a cell: hypertonic (more solute outside, cell shrinks), hypotonic (less solute outside, cell swells), and isotonic (equal concentrations, no net water movement). In plant cells, the cell wall prevents lysis in hypotonic solutions, maintaining turgor pressure. Water potential (Psi = Psi_p + Psi_s) governs the direction of water movement: water moves from high to low water potential. Students must calculate water potential and predict water movement. Connect to kidney function, plant transpiration, and contractile vacuoles in protists. AP exam note: water potential calculations and experimental design with potato cores are common. Vocabulary: tonicity, hypertonic, hypotonic, isotonic, plasmolysis, turgor, water potential, solute potential, pressure potential.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.G (Unit 2, Topic 2.9) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.9 Mechanisms of Transport
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers mechanisms of transport for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.G, Unit 2, Topic 2.9). Students should understand that active transport mechanisms use cellular energy to move substances against their concentration gradients. Primary active transport directly uses ATP (e.g., Na+/K+ ATPase). Secondary active transport (cotransport) uses the electrochemical gradient established by primary active transport to move other substances via symporters or antiporters. Bulk transport moves large molecules or particles: endocytosis brings material in, exocytosis releases material. Receptor-mediated endocytosis uses specific receptors for targeted uptake. Students must compare all transport mechanisms and explain energy requirements. Connect to neurotransmitter release, hormone secretion, and nutrient absorption. AP exam note: questions may require identifying transport type from experimental data. Vocabulary: primary active transport, cotransport, symport, antiport, endocytosis, exocytosis, receptor-mediated endocytosis.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: ENE-2.H (Unit 2, Topic 2.10) Cell Structure and Function - ENE 2.10 Cell Compartmentalization
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers cell compartmentalization for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-2.H, Unit 2, Topic 2.10). Students should understand that membrane-bound organelles create distinct chemical environments within eukaryotic cells, allowing incompatible metabolic processes to occur simultaneously. For example, lysosomes maintain an acidic pH for hydrolytic enzymes separate from the neutral cytoplasm. The ER and Golgi form an endomembrane system for protein processing and trafficking. Mitochondrial and chloroplast membranes create compartments essential for chemiosmosis. Students must explain how compartmentalization increases metabolic efficiency and enables cellular specialization. Connect to the evolution of eukaryotic cells (endosymbiotic theory) and to how disruption of compartmentalization causes disease. AP exam note: questions may ask why certain reactions must be separated. Vocabulary: compartmentalization, endomembrane system, chemiosmosis, lysosome, peroxisome, intermembrane space, matrix.

Cell Structure and Function.skill: EVO-2.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.11) Cell Structure and Function - EVO Origins of Cell Compartmentalization
Cell Structure and Function.prompt: This topic covers the origins of cell compartmentalization for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-2.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.11). Students should understand the endosymbiotic theory, which proposes that mitochondria and chloroplasts originated as free-living prokaryotes engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells. Evidence includes: both organelles have double membranes, their own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes (similar to bacteria), and they replicate by binary fission independently of the cell cycle. Mitochondria likely descended from aerobic proteobacteria, and chloroplasts from photosynthetic cyanobacteria. Students must evaluate evidence for endosymbiosis and explain how this theory accounts for structural features of these organelles. Connect to evolution, phylogenetics, and the diversity of eukaryotic cells. AP exam note: questions may present data and ask students to support or refute endosymbiotic theory. Vocabulary: endosymbiosis, endosymbiotic theory, double membrane, circular DNA, binary fission, cyanobacteria, proteobacteria.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Cellular Energetics - ENE Enzyme Structure
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers enzyme structure for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that enzymes are biological catalysts, mostly proteins, that lower activation energy without being consumed. Each enzyme has an active site with a specific 3D shape complementary to its substrate (induced fit model). Enzyme specificity arises from the shape and chemical properties of the active site. Cofactors (metal ions) and coenzymes (organic molecules like vitamins) assist enzyme function. Students must explain how enzyme structure determines substrate specificity and catalytic activity. Connect to protein structure (tertiary and quaternary), metabolism, and regulation of biochemical pathways. AP exam note: questions may ask students to interpret enzyme-substrate interaction diagrams or predict effects of structural changes. Vocabulary: enzyme, substrate, active site, activation energy, catalyst, cofactor, coenzyme, induced fit model.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Cellular Energetics - ENE 3.2 Enzyme Catalysis
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers enzyme catalysis for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand that enzymes increase reaction rates by lowering the activation energy barrier. The enzyme-substrate complex forms at the active site, where the enzyme stabilizes the transition state. Reaction rate depends on substrate concentration, enzyme concentration, and environmental conditions. At high substrate concentrations, enzymes become saturated (Vmax). Students must interpret graphs of reaction rate vs. substrate concentration and identify Vmax and Km (Michaelis constant). Competitive inhibitors bind the active site; noncompetitive (allosteric) inhibitors bind elsewhere and change enzyme shape. Connect to metabolic regulation, drug design, and feedback inhibition. AP exam note: data analysis of enzyme kinetics graphs is common. Vocabulary: activation energy, transition state, Vmax, Km, competitive inhibition, noncompetitive inhibition, allosteric regulation, enzyme-substrate complex.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) Cellular Energetics - ENE 3.3 Environmental Impacts on Enzyme Function
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers environmental impacts on enzyme function for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that enzyme activity is affected by temperature, pH, and substrate concentration. Each enzyme has an optimal temperature and pH at which activity is maximized. Temperatures or pH values outside the optimal range cause denaturation, unfolding the protein and destroying the active site shape. Most human enzymes function optimally near 37C and pH 7, but exceptions exist (pepsin at pH 2, taq polymerase at high temperatures). Students must interpret graphs showing enzyme activity vs. temperature or pH and explain results. Connect to homeostasis, adaptation to extreme environments, and biotechnology applications. AP exam note: experimental design questions may ask students to test optimal conditions. Vocabulary: optimal temperature, optimal pH, denaturation, thermophile, enzyme activity curve.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.D (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) Cellular Energetics - ENE 3.4 Cellular Energy
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers cellular energy for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the primary energy currency of cells. Energy is released when the terminal phosphate bond is hydrolyzed (ATP to ADP + Pi), and this energy drives endergonic reactions through energy coupling. ATP is continuously recycled: cells regenerate ATP from ADP through cellular respiration and photosynthesis. Students must explain the relationship between exergonic and endergonic reactions and how ATP couples them. Chemiosmosis, the flow of H+ ions through ATP synthase, is the primary mechanism of ATP production in both mitochondria and chloroplasts. Connect to metabolism, active transport, and muscle contraction. AP exam note: understanding the ATP cycle and energy coupling is fundamental. Vocabulary: ATP, ADP, phosphorylation, energy coupling, exergonic, endergonic, chemiosmosis, ATP synthase, free energy.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.E (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) Cellular Energetics - ENE 3.5 Photosynthesis
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers photosynthesis for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.E, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand the two stages: light-dependent reactions (in thylakoid membranes) and the Calvin cycle (in the stroma). Light reactions capture solar energy using chlorophyll and other pigments, split water (photolysis), generate ATP via chemiosmosis, and produce NADPH. The Calvin cycle uses ATP and NADPH to fix CO2 into G3P through carbon fixation (rubisco), reduction, and regeneration of RuBP. Overall equation: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy -> C6H12O6 + 6O2. Students must trace energy flow from light to chemical bonds and explain how environmental factors (light intensity, CO2 concentration, temperature) affect rate. Connect to cellular respiration, carbon cycle, and climate change. AP exam note: free-response questions on photosynthesis are very common. Vocabulary: chlorophyll, thylakoid, stroma, photosystem, electron transport chain, Calvin cycle, carbon fixation, rubisco, NADPH, photolysis.

Cellular Energetics.skill: ENE-3.F (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) Cellular Energetics - ENE 3.6 Cellular Respiration
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers cellular respiration for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-3.F, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand the three stages: glycolysis (cytoplasm), the Krebs cycle (mitochondrial matrix), and oxidative phosphorylation (inner mitochondrial membrane). Glycolysis splits glucose into two pyruvate molecules, producing 2 ATP and 2 NADH. The Krebs cycle oxidizes acetyl-CoA, generating CO2, NADH, FADH2, and GTP. Oxidative phosphorylation uses the electron transport chain to create a proton gradient driving ATP synthase (chemiosmosis), producing most of the approximately 30-32 ATP per glucose. Fermentation (anaerobic) regenerates NAD+ when oxygen is absent. Overall equation: C6H12O6 + 6O2 -> 6CO2 + 6H2O + ATP. Students must trace carbon, hydrogen, and energy through each stage. Connect to photosynthesis, exercise physiology, and evolution. AP exam note: detailed knowledge of each stage is expected. Vocabulary: glycolysis, pyruvate, acetyl-CoA, Krebs cycle, ETC, chemiosmosis, oxidative phosphorylation, fermentation, NAD+, FADH2.

Cellular Energetics.skill: SYI-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) Cellular Energetics - SYI 3.7 Fitness
Cellular Energetics.prompt: This topic covers fitness for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that biological fitness refers to an organism's ability to survive and reproduce in a given environment, not physical strength. Fitness is measured by reproductive success and the contribution of alleles to the next generation. Organisms with higher fitness have traits better suited to their environment, enabling them to obtain resources, avoid predation, and successfully reproduce. Students must distinguish between fitness in everyday language and evolutionary fitness. Natural selection acts on phenotypic variation within populations, favoring traits that increase fitness. Connect to natural selection, adaptation, and population genetics. AP exam note: questions may present data on survival and reproduction rates and ask students to determine relative fitness. Vocabulary: fitness, reproductive success, adaptation, natural selection, phenotype, allele frequency, relative fitness, selective advantage.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST 4.1 Cell Communication
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers cell communication for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that cells communicate through chemical signaling. Types of signaling include direct contact (gap junctions, plasmodesmata), paracrine (local signaling), endocrine (hormones through bloodstream), and autocrine (signaling to self). The three stages of cell signaling are reception (ligand binds receptor), transduction (signal relay through molecules), and response (cellular activity changes). Students must identify signaling types and explain how the same signal can produce different responses in different cell types due to different receptors and transduction pathways. Connect to nervous system signaling, immune responses, and development. AP exam note: questions may require comparing signaling types and explaining specificity. Vocabulary: ligand, receptor, signal transduction, paracrine, endocrine, autocrine, gap junction, plasmodesmata.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST 4.2 Introduction to Signal Transduction
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic introduces signal transduction for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that signal transduction pathways convert extracellular signals into intracellular responses through a cascade of molecular interactions. Key components include membrane receptors (G protein-coupled receptors, receptor tyrosine kinases, ion channel receptors), relay molecules, and second messengers (cAMP, Ca2+, IP3). Phosphorylation cascades (kinase cascades) amplify the signal at each step. Students must explain how signal amplification allows a small number of signaling molecules to produce a large cellular response. Connect to enzyme regulation, gene expression, and cellular responses like apoptosis. AP exam note: diagram interpretation of transduction pathways is common. Vocabulary: G protein, kinase, phosphorylation, second messenger, cAMP, signal amplification, receptor tyrosine kinase.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.C (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST 4.3 Signal Transduction
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers signal transduction in detail for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand the complete signaling pathway from reception through response. Intracellular receptors bind lipid-soluble signals (steroid hormones) that cross the membrane and directly activate gene expression. Membrane receptors bind water-soluble signals and relay information through transduction cascades. Responses include changes in gene expression, enzyme activity, or cell metabolism. Protein phosphatases remove phosphate groups to terminate signaling. Students must trace a signal from ligand binding through cellular response and explain how specificity and regulation are maintained. Connect to hormonal regulation, immune response, and cancer. AP exam note: free-response questions may ask students to explain consequences of pathway disruption. Vocabulary: intracellular receptor, phosphatase, transcription factor, gene expression, apoptosis, cascade, termination.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.4) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST Changes in Signal Transduction Pathways
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers changes in signal transduction pathways for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that mutations or changes in any component of a signaling pathway can alter cellular responses and lead to disease. Oncogenes (overactive growth signals) and tumor suppressor genes (lost growth inhibition) illustrate how signaling errors cause cancer. Changes can include constitutively active receptors, overexpression of growth factors, or loss of apoptotic signaling. Students must explain how disruptions at different points in a pathway produce different outcomes and why multiple mutations are typically required for cancer. Connect to genetic mutations, evolution, and drug targeting of signaling pathways. AP exam note: questions may present scenarios with altered signaling and ask for predicted outcomes. Vocabulary: oncogene, tumor suppressor, proto-oncogene, apoptosis, constitutive activation, mutation, carcinogenesis.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: ENE-4.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.5) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - ENE 4.5 Feedback
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers feedback mechanisms for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Students should understand that negative feedback loops maintain homeostasis by reversing a change from a set point (e.g., thermoregulation, blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon). Positive feedback loops amplify a response until a specific endpoint is reached (e.g., oxytocin during childbirth, blood clotting cascade, action potential depolarization). Most biological systems use negative feedback to maintain stable internal conditions. Students must diagram feedback loops, identify components (stimulus, receptor, control center, effector), and predict outcomes of disruptions. Connect to endocrine system, nervous system, and ecological stability. AP exam note: questions frequently ask students to distinguish between positive and negative feedback and apply them to novel scenarios. Vocabulary: homeostasis, negative feedback, positive feedback, set point, stimulus, receptor, effector, thermoregulation.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.E (Unit 4, Topic 4.6) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST 4.6 Cell Cycle
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers the cell cycle for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Students should understand the stages of the cell cycle: interphase (G1, S, G2) and mitotic phase (mitosis and cytokinesis). During G1, cells grow and perform normal functions. During S phase, DNA replicates. During G2, cells prepare for division. Mitosis consists of prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, producing two genetically identical daughter cells. Cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm (cleavage furrow in animals, cell plate in plants). Some cells exit the cycle into G0 phase. Students must describe events at each stage and explain the importance of accurate DNA replication and chromosome segregation. Connect to cancer, development, and tissue repair. AP exam note: microscopy-based questions identifying cell cycle stages are common. Vocabulary: interphase, mitosis, cytokinesis, chromosome, chromatid, centromere, spindle fiber, G0.

Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.skill: IST-4.F (Unit 4, Topic 4.7) Cell Communication and Cell Cycle - IST 4.7 Regulation of Cell Cycle
Cell Communication and Cell Cycle.prompt: This topic covers regulation of the cell cycle for AP Biology students (CED: IST-4.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). Students should understand that the cell cycle is regulated at checkpoints: G1/S (restriction point), G2/M, and the spindle assembly checkpoint in metaphase. Cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs) are key regulatory proteins; cyclin levels fluctuate during the cycle, activating CDKs at appropriate times. Internal signals (DNA damage, incomplete replication) and external signals (growth factors, density-dependent inhibition, anchorage dependence) control progression. Loss of cell cycle control leads to unregulated division and cancer. Students must explain how checkpoints prevent errors and how mutations in regulatory genes cause cancer. Connect to signal transduction, apoptosis, and cancer biology. AP exam note: questions may present data on cyclin levels or mutant cells. Vocabulary: checkpoint, cyclin, CDK, growth factor, apoptosis, tumor suppressor, p53, density-dependent inhibition.

Heredity.prompt: This topic covers meiosis for AP Biology students (CED: IST-5.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that meiosis is a type of cell division that produces four genetically unique haploid gametes from one diploid parent cell through two rounds of division (meiosis I and II). Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (reductional division), while meiosis II separates sister chromatids (equational division, similar to mitosis). Key events include synapsis and crossing over during prophase I, and independent assortment during metaphase I. Students must compare meiosis to mitosis, track chromosome number changes, and explain how meiosis produces genetic variation. Connect to sexual reproduction, Mendelian genetics, and evolution. AP exam note: questions frequently compare mitosis and meiosis or ask about ploidy changes. Vocabulary: meiosis, homologous chromosomes, synapsis, crossing over, independent assortment, haploid, diploid, gamete, tetrad, chiasma.

Heredity.skill: IST-5.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Heredity - IST 5.2 Meiosis and Genetic Diversity
Heredity.prompt: This topic covers meiosis and genetic diversity for AP Biology students (CED: IST-5.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand three mechanisms by which meiosis generates genetic variation: crossing over (recombination during prophase I exchanges segments between homologs), independent assortment (random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I produces 2^n possible gamete combinations), and random fertilization (any sperm can fuse with any egg). These mechanisms ensure that offspring are genetically unique, providing raw material for natural selection. Students must calculate the number of possible gamete combinations and explain how each mechanism contributes to variation. Connect to evolution, population genetics, and adaptation. AP exam note: mathematical reasoning about genetic diversity is expected. Vocabulary: crossing over, recombination, independent assortment, random fertilization, genetic variation, chiasma, recombinant chromosome.

Heredity.skill: EVO-5.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Heredity - EVO 5.3 Mendelian Genetics
Heredity.prompt: This topic covers Mendelian genetics for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-5.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand Mendel's laws: the Law of Segregation (two alleles for each gene separate during gamete formation) and the Law of Independent Assortment (genes on different chromosomes assort independently). Key concepts include dominant and recessive alleles, homozygous and heterozygous genotypes, phenotype vs. genotype, and Punnett squares for predicting offspring ratios. Monohybrid crosses yield 3:1 phenotypic ratios; dihybrid crosses yield 9:3:3:1. Test crosses determine unknown genotypes. Students must solve genetics problems and use probability rules (multiplication and addition). Connect to meiosis, chromosomal basis of inheritance, and deviations from Mendelian patterns. AP exam note: genetics problem-solving is heavily tested. Vocabulary: allele, genotype, phenotype, dominant, recessive, homozygous, heterozygous, Punnett square, test cross, monohybrid, dihybrid.

Heredity.skill: IST-5.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Heredity - IST 5.4 Non-Mendelian Genetics
Heredity.prompt: This topic covers non-Mendelian genetics for AP Biology students (CED: IST-5.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand inheritance patterns that deviate from simple Mendelian ratios: incomplete dominance (blended phenotype, 1:2:1 ratio), codominance (both alleles expressed, e.g., AB blood type), multiple alleles (e.g., ABO blood groups), polygenic inheritance (multiple genes affect one trait, producing continuous variation), pleiotropy (one gene affects multiple traits, e.g., sickle cell), and epistasis (one gene masks another). Sex-linked inheritance involves genes on X or Y chromosomes. Students must solve problems involving these patterns and recognize them from pedigrees and data. Connect to genetic diversity, evolution, and human genetic disorders. AP exam note: pedigree analysis and non-Mendelian crosses are common. Vocabulary: incomplete dominance, codominance, polygenic, pleiotropy, epistasis, sex-linked, X-linked, pedigree, carrier.

Heredity.skill: SYI-5.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.5) Heredity - SYI 5.5 Environmental Effects on Phenotype
Heredity.prompt: This topic covers environmental effects on phenotype for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-5.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand that phenotype results from the interaction between genotype and environment. Environmental factors such as temperature, nutrition, light, and chemical exposure can influence gene expression and phenotypic outcomes. Examples include temperature-sensitive coat color in Siamese cats, the effect of soil pH on hydrangea flower color, and height variation due to nutrition. Phenotypic plasticity describes an organism's ability to change phenotype in response to environment. Identical twins raised in different environments may show phenotypic differences despite identical genotypes. Students must distinguish genetic from environmental influences on phenotype. Connect to epigenetics, norms of reaction, and polygenic traits. AP exam note: questions may present twin studies or environmental manipulation data. Vocabulary: phenotypic plasticity, norm of reaction, epigenetics, multifactorial, gene-environment interaction.

Heredity.skill: SYI-5.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.6) Heredity - SYI 5.6 Chromosomal Inheritance
Heredity.prompt: This topic covers chromosomal inheritance for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-5.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand that genes located on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together (linked genes), unless separated by crossing over. Recombination frequency between linked genes can be used to map relative gene positions on chromosomes. Chromosomal abnormalities include nondisjunction (producing aneuploid gametes with extra or missing chromosomes, e.g., trisomy 21), deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations. Sex determination in humans depends on X and Y chromosomes. Students must solve linkage mapping problems and predict outcomes of nondisjunction. Connect to meiotic errors, genetic disorders, and karyotype analysis. AP exam note: questions may involve interpreting karyotypes or calculating recombination frequencies. Vocabulary: linked genes, recombination frequency, nondisjunction, aneuploidy, trisomy, monosomy, deletion, translocation, karyotype.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.1 DNA and RNA Structure
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers DNA and RNA structure for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand the structural differences between DNA and RNA: DNA is double-stranded with deoxyribose sugar, uses thymine, and forms a stable double helix for long-term information storage. RNA is single-stranded with ribose sugar, uses uracil, and exists in multiple forms (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA) for different functions. Nucleotides are linked by phosphodiester bonds in a 5-prime to 3-prime direction. The antiparallel orientation and complementary base pairing (A-T/U, G-C) are essential for replication and transcription. Students must compare DNA and RNA structures and explain how structure relates to function. Connect to replication, transcription, gene regulation, and biotechnology. AP exam note: diagram-based questions on nucleic acid structure are common. Vocabulary: deoxyribose, ribose, phosphodiester bond, antiparallel, complementary base pairing, double helix.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST Replication
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers DNA replication for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that replication is semiconservative: each new DNA molecule contains one original and one new strand. Key enzymes include helicase (unwinds double helix), primase (synthesizes RNA primers), DNA polymerase III (adds nucleotides 5-prime to 3-prime, proofreads), and ligase (joins Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand). The leading strand is synthesized continuously; the lagging strand is synthesized in Okazaki fragments. Origins of replication allow bidirectional replication. Telomerase extends telomeres in germ cells and stem cells, preventing chromosome shortening. Students must describe the replication process and explain why it is semiconservative. Connect to cell division, mutations, aging, and cancer. AP exam note: Meselson-Stahl experiment evidence is important. Vocabulary: semiconservative, helicase, primase, DNA polymerase, ligase, Okazaki fragment, leading strand, lagging strand, telomere, telomerase.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.3 Transcription and RNA Processing
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers transcription and RNA processing for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that transcription produces mRNA from a DNA template using RNA polymerase, which reads the template strand 3-prime to 5-prime and synthesizes mRNA 5-prime to 3-prime. In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: addition of a 5-prime cap (modified guanine), a 3-prime poly-A tail, and removal of introns by spliceosomes (RNA splicing), leaving exons joined together. Alternative splicing allows one gene to produce multiple proteins. In prokaryotes, mRNA is not processed and translation can begin before transcription finishes. Students must compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic transcription and explain the significance of RNA processing. Connect to gene regulation, protein diversity, and evolution. AP exam note: questions on RNA processing steps are common. Vocabulary: RNA polymerase, template strand, coding strand, promoter, intron, exon, spliceosome, alternative splicing, 5-prime cap, poly-A tail.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.D (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST Translation
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers translation for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that translation converts mRNA codons into an amino acid sequence (polypeptide) at ribosomes. Transfer RNA (tRNA) carries amino acids and has anticodons complementary to mRNA codons. The genetic code is universal, redundant (degenerate), and non-overlapping. Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added via peptide bonds at A, P, E sites), and termination (stop codon reached, polypeptide released). Students must use a codon chart to determine amino acid sequences and predict effects of mutations on protein structure. Connect to mutations, gene expression, and post-translational modification. AP exam note: codon chart interpretation and mutation analysis are heavily tested. Vocabulary: codon, anticodon, ribosome, tRNA, mRNA, start codon, stop codon, peptide bond, A site, P site, E site, polypeptide.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.E (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.5 Regulation of Gene Expression
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers regulation of gene expression for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that gene expression is regulated at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (epigenetic modifications like DNA methylation and histone acetylation), transcriptional regulation (transcription factors, enhancers, silencers, promoters), post-transcriptional regulation (alternative splicing, mRNA stability, miRNA), translational regulation, and post-translational modification (phosphorylation, ubiquitination). In prokaryotes, operons (lac and trp) coordinate gene regulation. In eukaryotes, regulation is more complex due to chromatin structure and long-range regulatory elements. Students must explain how differential gene expression leads to cell specialization despite identical DNA. Connect to development, cancer, and epigenetics. AP exam note: operon models and eukaryotic regulation are heavily tested. Vocabulary: operon, promoter, operator, repressor, activator, enhancer, epigenetic, methylation, histone, transcription factor.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.F (Unit 6, Topic 6.6) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.6 Gene Expression and Cell Specialization
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers gene expression and cell specialization for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that all cells in a multicellular organism contain the same DNA, but differential gene expression determines cell type and function. During development, cells become specialized through the selective activation and silencing of genes controlled by transcription factors, signaling molecules, and epigenetic modifications. Examples include muscle cells expressing myosin genes and red blood cells expressing hemoglobin genes. Stem cells retain the ability to differentiate into multiple cell types. Induction by neighboring cells influences cell fate during embryonic development. Students must explain how gene regulation produces diverse cell types. Connect to signal transduction, development, and regenerative medicine. AP exam note: questions may present gene expression data from different tissues. Vocabulary: differentiation, cell specialization, stem cell, totipotent, pluripotent, induction, morphogen, homeotic gene.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.G (Unit 6, Topic 6.7) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.7 Mutations
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers mutations for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.G, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that mutations are changes in DNA sequence that can be spontaneous or induced by mutagens (UV light, chemicals, radiation). Types include point mutations: substitutions (silent, missense, nonsense) and insertions/deletions (causing frameshifts). Chromosomal mutations include large-scale deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations. Some mutations are beneficial (providing variation for natural selection), some are neutral (due to redundancy in the genetic code), and some are harmful (causing genetic disorders or cancer). Students must predict the effect of specific mutations on protein structure and function using the codon chart. Connect to evolution, cancer, genetic disorders, and gene regulation. AP exam note: mutation analysis with codon charts is very common. Vocabulary: point mutation, substitution, insertion, deletion, frameshift, silent, missense, nonsense, mutagen, carcinogen.

Gene Expression and Regulation.skill: IST-6.H (Unit 6, Topic 6.8) Gene Expression and Regulation - IST 6.8 Biotechnology
Gene Expression and Regulation.prompt: This topic covers biotechnology for AP Biology students (CED: IST-6.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand key biotechnology techniques: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) amplifies specific DNA segments using primers, Taq polymerase, and thermal cycling. Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size (smaller fragments migrate farther). Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences, enabling recombinant DNA technology. Bacterial transformation introduces foreign DNA via plasmid vectors. DNA sequencing determines nucleotide order. CRISPR-Cas9 allows precise genome editing. Applications include genetic engineering, gene therapy, forensics, and GMO production. Students must describe techniques and analyze gel electrophoresis results. Connect to gene expression, cloning, and ethical considerations. AP exam note: interpreting gel electrophoresis and experimental design with biotechnology tools are common. Vocabulary: PCR, gel electrophoresis, restriction enzyme, plasmid, recombinant DNA, transformation, CRISPR, GMO, DNA fingerprinting.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.1) Natural Selection - EVO 7.1 Introduction to Natural Selection
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic introduces natural selection for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand Darwin's theory: natural selection acts on heritable phenotypic variation within populations. Individuals with traits better suited to their environment have higher fitness (greater reproductive success), passing advantageous alleles to offspring. Over generations, this changes allele frequencies in the population. Four conditions are required: variation exists, variation is heritable, more offspring are produced than can survive, and survival and reproduction are not random. Natural selection acts on individuals but evolution occurs in populations. Students must distinguish natural selection from other evolutionary mechanisms. Connect to evidence of evolution, adaptation, and population genetics. AP exam note: questions require applying natural selection to novel scenarios. Vocabulary: natural selection, fitness, adaptation, heritable variation, differential reproduction, population, allele frequency.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.2) Natural Selection - EVO Natural Selection
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers natural selection mechanisms in depth for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand the three modes of natural selection: directional selection (favors one extreme phenotype, shifts distribution), stabilizing selection (favors intermediate phenotype, narrows distribution), and disruptive selection (favors both extremes, widens distribution). Sexual selection is a form of natural selection driven by mate choice (intersexual selection) or competition for mates (intrasexual selection). Students must interpret graphs showing phenotype distribution before and after selection and identify the selection mode. Natural selection can only act on existing variation and does not produce perfection. Connect to population genetics, speciation, and Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. AP exam note: graph interpretation of selection modes is commonly tested. Vocabulary: directional selection, stabilizing selection, disruptive selection, sexual selection, phenotype distribution, selective pressure.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.C (Unit 7, Topic 7.3) Natural Selection - EVO 7.3 Artificial Selection
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers artificial selection for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that artificial selection occurs when humans selectively breed organisms for desired traits, leading to rapid phenotypic change. Examples include domesticated dogs from wolves, crop plants (corn from teosinte), and livestock breeding for milk or meat production. Artificial selection demonstrates that heritable variation exists and that selection can drive significant change, supporting Darwin's theory. However, artificial selection can reduce genetic diversity and create health problems (e.g., hip dysplasia in dogs). Students must compare artificial and natural selection, noting that both act on heritable variation but differ in the selecting agent. Connect to biotechnology, GMOs, and conservation genetics. AP exam note: questions may ask students to use artificial selection as evidence for evolution. Vocabulary: artificial selection, selective breeding, domestication, genetic diversity, trait, heritable variation.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.D (Unit 7, Topic 7.4) Natural Selection - EVO 7.4 Population Genetics
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers population genetics for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.D, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand that evolution occurs at the population level through changes in allele frequencies. Mechanisms of evolution include natural selection, genetic drift (bottleneck effect, founder effect), gene flow (migration), and mutation. Genetic drift has a stronger effect in small populations and is random, unlike natural selection. Gene flow introduces new alleles through migration between populations, increasing variation within and decreasing variation between populations. Mutation is the ultimate source of all new alleles. Students must predict how each mechanism affects allele frequencies and genetic variation. Connect to Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, speciation, and conservation biology. AP exam note: distinguishing mechanisms of evolution is heavily tested. Vocabulary: allele frequency, genetic drift, bottleneck effect, founder effect, gene flow, mutation, population genetics, microevolution.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.E (Unit 7, Topic 7.5) Natural Selection - EVO Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.E, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand that Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes a non-evolving population where allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations. Five conditions must be met: no mutation, random mating, no natural selection, extremely large population size, and no gene flow. The equations are p + q = 1 (allele frequencies) and p-squared + 2pq + q-squared = 1 (genotype frequencies), where p = dominant allele frequency and q = recessive allele frequency. Students must calculate allele and genotype frequencies from population data and determine whether a population is in equilibrium. Deviations indicate evolution is occurring. Connect to population genetics, natural selection, and genetic drift. AP exam note: Hardy-Weinberg calculations are very commonly tested. Vocabulary: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, allele frequency, genotype frequency, equilibrium conditions, evolving population.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.F (Unit 7, Topic 7.6) Natural Selection - EVO 7.6 Evidence of Evolution
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers evidence of evolution for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand multiple lines of evidence supporting evolution: the fossil record shows transitional forms and extinction patterns; biogeography explains species distribution based on geological history; comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures (shared ancestry) vs. analogous structures (convergent evolution) and vestigial structures; comparative embryology shows similar developmental stages among related species; molecular evidence (DNA and protein sequence comparisons) shows that closely related species have more similar sequences. Students must evaluate evidence and construct arguments for common ancestry. Connect to phylogenetics, speciation, and classification. AP exam note: questions may present molecular or anatomical data to support evolutionary relationships. Vocabulary: fossil record, homologous, analogous, vestigial, biogeography, comparative anatomy, molecular homology, convergent evolution.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.G (Unit 7, Topic 7.7) Natural Selection - EVO Common Ancestry
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers common ancestry for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.G, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand that all life shares common ancestry, supported by universal features: DNA as genetic material, the universal genetic code, shared metabolic pathways (glycolysis), conserved genes (e.g., Hox genes), and similar cellular structures. Molecular comparisons provide quantitative evidence: species with more similar DNA or protein sequences diverged more recently. Phylogenetic trees depict evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characteristics. Molecular clocks use mutation rates to estimate divergence times. Students must use molecular data to support claims of common ancestry and construct or interpret phylogenetic trees. Connect to classification, speciation, and biodiversity. AP exam note: cladogram interpretation is frequently tested. Vocabulary: common ancestor, phylogenetic tree, cladogram, molecular clock, conserved sequence, homology, divergence, universal genetic code.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.H (Unit 7, Topic 7.8) Natural Selection - EVO 7.8 Continuing Evolution
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers continuing evolution for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.H, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand that evolution is an ongoing process observable in real time. Examples include antibiotic resistance in bacteria (natural selection favoring resistant mutants), pesticide resistance in insects, evolution of beak size in Galapagos finches in response to drought, and the rise of drug-resistant HIV. These examples demonstrate that environmental changes create new selective pressures, driving rapid evolution in populations with short generation times. Students must explain how human activities accelerate evolution in other species and why resistance evolves. Connect to natural selection, mutation, population genetics, and public health. AP exam note: questions may present data on resistance evolution and ask for explanations using evolutionary principles. Vocabulary: antibiotic resistance, selective pressure, adaptation, microevolution, generation time, resistant allele, overuse of antibiotics.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.I (Unit 7, Topic 7.9) Natural Selection - EVO Phylogeny
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers phylogeny for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.I, Unit 7, Topic 7.9). Students should understand that phylogenetic trees (cladograms) represent hypotheses about evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies). Cladograms show branching patterns: nodes represent common ancestors, branch tips represent taxa, and outgroups root the tree. Phylogenies can be constructed using morphological data, molecular sequences, or both. Maximum parsimony and molecular clock analyses are used to build and evaluate trees. Students must interpret cladograms to determine relationships, identify common ancestors, and infer traits of ancestral species. Connect to classification, evidence of evolution, and molecular biology. AP exam note: cladogram reading and construction are core skills. Vocabulary: phylogeny, cladogram, node, branch, outgroup, synapomorphy, monophyletic, parsimony, taxon, derived character.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.J (Unit 7, Topic 7.10) Natural Selection - EVO Speciation
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers speciation for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.J, Unit 7, Topic 7.10). Students should understand that speciation is the formation of new species when populations become reproductively isolated. Allopatric speciation occurs when geographic barriers physically separate populations (e.g., mountain ranges, rivers), and genetic divergence accumulates over time. Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic separation, often through polyploidy (especially in plants) or habitat differentiation. Reproductive isolation mechanisms include prezygotic barriers (temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic isolation) and postzygotic barriers (hybrid inviability, sterility, breakdown). Students must distinguish speciation types and identify isolation mechanisms. Connect to natural selection, genetic drift, and biodiversity. AP exam note: scenarios requiring identification of speciation type are common. Vocabulary: speciation, allopatric, sympatric, reproductive isolation, prezygotic, postzygotic, polyploidy, geographic barrier.

Natural Selection.skill: EVO-7.K (Unit 7, Topic 7.11) Natural Selection - EVO Extinction
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers extinction for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.K, Unit 7, Topic 7.11). Students should understand that extinction occurs when a species can no longer survive and reproduce in its environment. Causes include environmental change, competition, disease, predation, and catastrophic events. Mass extinctions have occurred five times in Earth's history, each followed by adaptive radiation as surviving lineages diversify into newly available niches. The current biodiversity crisis is driven by human activities: habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Background extinction is the normal rate of species loss between mass extinction events. Students must explain how extinction affects biodiversity and ecosystem function. Connect to natural selection, ecological succession, and conservation biology. AP exam note: questions may link extinction to adaptive radiation or human impact. Vocabulary: extinction, mass extinction, adaptive radiation, background extinction, biodiversity, niche, endangered species.

Natural Selection.skill: SYI-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.12) Natural Selection - SYI 7.12 Variations in Populations
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers variations in populations for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.12). Students should understand that genetic variation within populations is maintained by several mechanisms: mutation introduces new alleles, sexual reproduction (meiosis, crossing over, random fertilization) shuffles existing alleles, and balancing selection (heterozygote advantage, frequency-dependent selection) preserves multiple alleles in a population. High genetic variation increases a population's ability to adapt to changing environments. Low variation (due to genetic drift, bottleneck, or inbreeding) reduces adaptive potential and increases extinction risk. Students must explain how variation is generated and maintained. Connect to Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, natural selection, and conservation genetics. AP exam note: questions may ask why maintaining genetic diversity matters. Vocabulary: genetic variation, heterozygote advantage, frequency-dependent selection, balancing selection, inbreeding, bottleneck, gene pool.

Natural Selection.skill: SYI-7.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.13) Natural Selection - SYI Origin of Life on Earth
Natural Selection.prompt: This topic covers the origin of life on Earth for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.13). Students should understand scientific hypotheses about life's origins: early Earth's atmosphere lacked O2 and contained reducing gases. The Miller-Urey experiment demonstrated that organic molecules (amino acids) could form abiotically under early Earth conditions. The RNA world hypothesis proposes that self-replicating RNA preceded DNA and proteins, as RNA can both store information and catalyze reactions (ribozymes). Protocells (lipid vesicles enclosing organic molecules) may have preceded true cells. The earliest fossil evidence of life dates to about 3.5 billion years ago (stromatolites). Students must evaluate evidence for each hypothesis. Connect to evolution, biochemistry, and astrobiology. AP exam note: questions may ask students to evaluate experimental evidence for abiogenesis. Vocabulary: abiogenesis, Miller-Urey experiment, RNA world, ribozyme, protocell, stromatolite, reducing atmosphere, organic molecule.

Ecology.skill: ENE-8.A (Unit 8, Topic 8.1) Ecology - ENE 8.1 Responses to the Environment
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers responses to the environment for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-8.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.1). Students should understand that organisms respond to environmental stimuli through behaviors and physiological mechanisms. Behavioral responses include taxis (directed movement toward or away from a stimulus), kinesis (undirected movement), and circadian rhythms. Physiological responses include hibernation, estivation, migration, and photoperiodism. Plants respond through tropisms (phototropism, gravitropism, thigmotropism) mediated by hormones such as auxin. Communication between organisms involves visual, auditory, chemical (pheromones), and tactile signals. Students must explain how responses increase fitness and reproductive success. Connect to natural selection, signal transduction, and ecology. AP exam note: questions may require explaining how specific behaviors are adaptive. Vocabulary: taxis, kinesis, tropism, photoperiodism, circadian rhythm, pheromone, auxin, innate behavior, learned behavior.

Ecology.skill: ENE-8.B (Unit 8, Topic 8.2) Ecology - ENE 8.2 Energy Flow Through Ecosystems
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers energy flow through ecosystems for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-8.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.2). Students should understand that energy flows through ecosystems in one direction: from producers to consumers to decomposers. Only about 10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels (10 percent rule); the rest is lost as heat through cellular respiration. Food chains and food webs illustrate feeding relationships. Ecological pyramids display energy, biomass, or numbers at each trophic level. Gross primary productivity (GPP) is total photosynthesis; net primary productivity (NPP = GPP minus respiration) is energy available to consumers. Students must calculate energy transfer between trophic levels and construct or interpret ecological pyramids. Connect to photosynthesis, respiration, and nutrient cycling. AP exam note: energy transfer calculations and pyramid interpretation are common. Vocabulary: trophic level, producer, consumer, decomposer, food web, GPP, NPP, biomass, energy pyramid.

Ecology.skill: SYI-8.A (Unit 8, Topic 8.3) Ecology - SYI 8.3 Population Ecology
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers population ecology for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-8.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.3). Students should understand that populations are characterized by size, density, dispersion (clumped, uniform, random), and age structure. Population growth models include exponential growth (J-curve, dN/dt = rN, unlimited resources) and logistic growth (S-curve, dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K, limited by carrying capacity K). Birth rate, death rate, immigration, and emigration determine population change. Age structure diagrams predict future growth trends. Students must interpret and compare growth curves, calculate growth rates, and use age structure diagrams. Connect to carrying capacity, limiting factors, and human population growth. AP exam note: mathematical modeling of population growth is heavily tested. Vocabulary: population density, carrying capacity, exponential growth, logistic growth, r, K, age structure, dispersion.

Ecology.skill: SYI-8.B (Unit 8, Topic 8.4) Ecology - SYI 8.4 Effect of Density of Populations
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers the effect of density on populations for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-8.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.4). Students should understand that density-dependent factors limit population growth as population size increases: competition for resources, predation, disease, parasitism, and waste accumulation intensify with higher density. Density-independent factors affect populations regardless of size: natural disasters, weather events, and seasonal changes. K-selected species have traits adapted for life near carrying capacity (low reproduction, long lifespan, high parental care), while r-selected species thrive in unstable environments (high reproduction, short lifespan, little parental care). Students must distinguish factor types and predict their effects on growth. Connect to logistic growth, carrying capacity, and community ecology. AP exam note: questions may present population data and ask which factors are operating. Vocabulary: density-dependent, density-independent, K-selected, r-selected, carrying capacity, intraspecific competition.

Ecology.skill: ENE-8.C (Unit 8, Topic 8.5) Ecology - ENE 8.5 Community Ecology
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers community ecology for AP Biology students (CED: ENE-8.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.5). Students should understand that communities consist of interacting populations. Key interactions include competition (interspecific, leading to competitive exclusion or resource partitioning), predation (drives coevolution of defenses and hunting strategies), mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, other harmed). Keystone species have disproportionate effects on community structure relative to their abundance. Ecological succession involves predictable community changes: primary succession (on bare substrate) and secondary succession (after disturbance). Students must identify interaction types and predict community responses to changes. Connect to biodiversity, evolution, and ecosystem stability. AP exam note: scenarios involving species interactions and succession are common. Vocabulary: niche, competitive exclusion, resource partitioning, keystone species, mutualism, commensalism, parasitism, ecological succession.

This topic covers biodiversity for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-8.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.6). Students should understand that biodiversity encompasses genetic diversity (variation within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance of species in a community), and ecosystem diversity (variety of ecosystems in a region). High biodiversity increases ecosystem resilience and stability. Species richness and relative abundance together determine species diversity (measured by indices like Shannon diversity). Biodiversity hotspots are regions with exceptionally high species diversity under threat. Threats include habitat loss, invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change. Students must explain why biodiversity matters for ecosystem function and human well-being. Connect to extinction, conservation, and ecological interactions. AP exam note: data interpretation of diversity indices may appear. Vocabulary: biodiversity, species richness, species evenness, ecosystem services, habitat fragmentation, invasive species, conservation biology.

Ecology.skill: EVO-8.A (Unit 8, Topic 8.7) Ecology - EVO 8.7 Disruptions to Ecosystems
Ecology.prompt: This topic covers disruptions to ecosystems for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-8.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.7). Students should understand that ecosystems can be disrupted by natural events (volcanic eruptions, fires, hurricanes) and human activities (deforestation, pollution, urbanization, introduction of invasive species, climate change). Disruptions alter species composition, energy flow, and nutrient cycling. Invasive species outcompete native species by lacking natural predators. Climate change affects habitats, migration patterns, and phenology. Biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water) can be disrupted by human input of pollutants and greenhouse gases. Ecosystems may recover through succession if the disruption is not too severe. Students must analyze the ecological consequences of specific disruptions. Connect to biodiversity, extinction, and sustainability. AP exam note: questions often present data on human impact and ask for analysis. Vocabulary: invasive species, climate change, greenhouse gas, biogeochemical cycle, eutrophication, deforestation, ecological footprint, sustainability.
